confirmed.summary: Leading-zero timeout values pass validation but break octal arithmetic, leaving SECS unset; under set -u every agent subshell then dies on 'SECS: unbound variable' (lines 109-121 validate, 117-118 convert, 198 expands "$SECS").
confirmed.scenario: CONFIRMED: `--timeout 09m` (or 08h) passes the case-based validation, then `SECS=$((09 * 60))` fails with '09: value too great for base' (bash treats 09 as octal); the script continues (no set -e), creates all worktrees/branches, launches every subshell, and each aborts at `timeout -k 10 "$SECS"` with set -u unbound-variable — 100% of agents report FAILED(exit) after branch pollution. Reproduced on bash 5.3.9.
votes: CONFIRMED by end-to-end reproduction on bash 5.3.9 against the actual script. `--timeout 09m` passes the case-based validation (lines 109-115 check only digit/suffix shape, not leading zeros). Line 118 then fails with '09: value too great for base (error token is "09")' because bash arithmetic treats the 0-prefixed operand as octal, leaving SECS unset; with `set -uo pipefail` but no `set -e`, the script continues. It created the worktree and branch kimi/brief1 (branch pollution), launched the agent subshell, which aborted at line 198 (`timeout -k 10 "$SECS"`) with 'SECS: unbound variable' before ever invoking kimi (verified via stub kimi that never ran). The run reported FAILED(exit) for 100% of agents and exited 1. Note: 01m-07m accidentally work (octal==decimal below 8); only 08/09-prefixed values trigger the failure, exactly as claimed.

CONFIRMED by end-to-end reproduction, not refuted. Verified empirically.

CONFIRMED empirically, not refuted. CONFIRMED empirically. In ${b%.*} the glob * matches '/', so for brief path 'a.dir/task' the shortest suffix matching '.*' is '.dir/task', making the dup-check name 'a' (and 'b' for 'b.dir/task'), while run_one (basename first, then %.*) names both agents 'task'. Reproduced end-to-end with a stub kimi: `kimi_parallel.sh --repo <repo> a.dir/task b.dir/task` passes the dup-check, both agents collide on name 'task' sharing task.log and branch kimi/task; the second worktree add fails ('branch kimi/task already exists'), the second brief is silently dropped, and the script reports '1/1 agent(s) succeeded' exit 0 — the exact collision (plus silent brief loss) the check was added to prevent. Fix: compute the check's name in the same order as run_one (n=$(basename "$b"); n=${n%.*}).

confirmed.summary: Timeout classification (line 246) only treats rc==124 as FAILED(timeout); when the -k 10 SIGKILL path fires (agent ignores/blocks SIGTERM), GNU timeout exits 137, so a genuine timeout is reported as FAILED(exit) — or FAILED(quota) if the log tail happens to match the quota regex.
confirmed.scenario: CONFIRMED empirically: subshell running `timeout -k 2 1 <TERM-ignoring child>` propagates rc=137 through wait (TERM-respecting child gives 124). A wedged kimi that doesn't die on TERM — the exact hang class the wrapper was added for — is misclassified. Fix: treat 124 and 137 (and optionally 143) as timeout.
votes: CONFIRMED, not refuted. Empirical test on this exact host (GNU coreutils timeout 9.11, Termux) replicating the script's structure — subshell running `timeout -k 2 1 <child>` with `exit $rc`, backgrounded, reaped via `wait $!` — yields rc=137 when the child ignores SIGTERM (trap '' TERM) and rc=124 when it doesn't. kimi_parallel.sh line 246 classifies only rc==124 as FAILED(timeout); rc=137 falls through to log_tail_matches_quota (line 248), so a wedged kimi that survives SIGTERM (the exact hang class the -k 10 wrapper at line 198 targets) is misreported as FAILED(exit), or as FAILED(quota) with a spurious quota-fallback hint if the log tail contains marker text like "termios" (in the regex at line 235). Proposed fix (treat 124 and 137, optionally 143, as timeout) is sound.

Defect is real and empirically reproduced on this host. The script wraps kimi in `timeout -k 10 "$SECS" ...` (line 198) and classifies only rc==124 as timeout (line 246). Test on the actual Termux GNU coreutils timeout 9.11, replicating the script's subshell/rc-capture/wait structure: a TERM-ignoring child (the wedged-process class -k 10 exists for) propagates rc=137 through `wait`, while a TERM-respecting child gives 124. Thus a genuine timeout in the SIGKILL path is reported FAILED(exit), or FAILED(quota) if the log tail matches the quota regex (setting quota_seen and printing the wrong fallback hint). Minor caveat: 137 is ambiguous with external SIGKILL (e.g. OOM), so treating 124|137 as timeout is a heuristic fix, but the misclassification claim itself stands.

NOT refuted — defect confirmed empirically on this exact host (Termux, GNU coreutils 9.11 timeout, same binary the script invokes). Reproduced the script's exact structure from /data/data/com.termux/files/home/.claude/skills/kimi-delegate/scripts/kimi_parallel.sh (line 198 `timeout -k 10 "$SECS" kimi ...` inside a background subshell that does `rc=$?; exit $rc`, then `wait "$PID"` at line 242): a TERM-ignoring child (`bash -c 'trap "" TERM; sleep 30'`) under `timeout -k 2 1` yields rc=137 both inside the subshell and through `wait`, while a TERM-respecting child yields 124. Since line 246 only checks `rc -eq 124`, the 137 case falls through to the quota-tail scan (lines 248-249) and then FAILED(exit) at line 252 — so a wedged kimi that ignores/blocks SIGTERM (the hang class the wrapper exists for, e.g. termios stalls) is reported as FAILED(exit), or FAILED(quota) if the last 20 log lines happen to match the quota regex (plausible, since a quota-stalled agent's log tail would contain quota text). One nuance for the fixer: rc=137 is not exclusively the -k path — an Android LMK/OOM SIGKILL of kimi also yields 137 — so classifying 137 as timeout is a heuristic; a stricter fix could label it FAILED(timeout|killed). But the claimed misclassification itself is real and demonstrated.

confirmed.summary: Validation accepts '0s'/'0m'/'0' and converts to SECS=0, which GNU timeout interprets as 'timeout disabled' — the wrapper documented as the ONLY hang protection is silently turned off by an input that looks valid.
confirmed.scenario: CONFIRMED conversion: `--timeout 0s` -> SECS=0 -> `timeout -k 10 0 kimi ...` never times out; a termios-hung kimi then hangs the run forever, the failure mode the flag was added to prevent. Reject 0 in validation (minor; only bites on explicit user input).

confirmed.summary: Timeout classification only checks rc==124; the SIGKILL grace path exits 137 and is misreported as FAILED(exit) or FAILED(quota). Line 246.
confirmed.scenario: Agent process (kimi is a Node CLI that can install a SIGTERM handler, or its event loop is wedged in a busy native call so the handler never dispatches) survives timeout's SIGTERM; after the -k 10 grace, KILL fires and GNU timeout exits 137, not 124. Empirically verified on this host (coreutils 9.5): a trap-'' TERM child through the exact subshell+wait construct yields rc=137 -> classified FAILED(exit). Idle hangs (blocked in read, default TERM disposition) correctly give 124, so precisely the busy/wedged hangs — the ones the wrapper exists for — lose their timeout root cause, and if the truncated log tail happens to contain a marker string they are further misreported FAILED(quota) and trigger the abandon-kimi hint. Fix: treat rc 124 AND 137 as timeout.

The script's own comment (lines 105-107) states this wrapper is "the ONLY hang protection" against documented kimi termios/quota stalls; with it disabled, a stalled agent subshell never exits and the parent blocks forever in 'wait "${PIDS[$i]}"' (line 242) with no other watchdog. The 0=infinite semantics are undocumented in the help text ("use e.g. 90s, 15m, 1h"), so this is a silent defeat of the flag's stated purpose, not an intentional escape hatch. Proposed fix (reject SECS -eq 0 after conversion) is correct.

confirmed.summary: Dup-name check computes the name as basename "${b%.*}" (strip-extension THEN basename) while run_one does basename THEN strip-extension; for extensionless briefs under a directory containing a dot the two diverge, producing both missed real collisions and false duplicate aborts. confirmed.scenario: Empirically verified both directions. False negative: 'kimi_parallel.sh --repo r briefs.v2/task ./task' — dup check compares 'briefs' vs 'task' ("${b%.*}" strips '.v2/task' at the path's last dot) and passes, but run_one names BOTH agents 'task': they append to the same $RESULTS_DIR/task.log interleaved and contend for branch kimi/task / the same worktree dir — exactly the corruption the check was added to block. confirmed.summary: Word-boundary separator list is ASCII-only; aliases adjacent to common Unicode punctuation (Bulgarian quotes „ “, em-dash —, ellipsis …, « ») no longer match, a recall regression vs the substring-matching pre version — and inflected Cyrillic forms (грешката, бъга) were dropped entirely. confirmed.scenario: Empirically verified against the live hook: prompt '„поправи грешката“ в скрипта моля' produces NO directive (token is '„поправи', and 'грешката' is not in the alias list — pre-change *поправи*/*грешка* substrings both matched); prompt 'the parser error—it loops forever' also silently passes ('error—it' is one token; pre matched *error*). Bulgarian orthography standardly uses „…“ quotes and the context mandates Cyrillic aliases keep matching. Fail-open is preserved (rc=0, telemetry 'pass' logged) so this is a silent coverage loss the soak will record as unmatched. Fix: add „ “ ” ‘ ’ — – … « » to the separator loop (bash ${p//…/ } handles multibyte fine) and add inflected Cyrillic aliases (грешката, бъга, бъгът). Note: state-reinject.sh's rm -f was also audited and has NO defect — the expansion is double-quoted so a SID of '*' globs nothing (verified), and traversal via '../' cannot resolve without a nonexistent 'task-gate-<x>' intermediate directory.
